Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'd block out time most days, usually around 24 hours a week total, and I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things or just focused listening sessions. The thing is, even though I put in the hours, I struggled to make it all stick for exams. I completed almost everything assigned, but my approach was probably more about getting through material than deeply understanding it.
Q2: My pattern was somewhat regular, but it definitely shifted based on what was due. I'd have my baseline study times, but when assignments were coming up, I'd intensify and spend more hours listening to lectures again or reviewing discussion posts. Deadlines created these spikes where I'd be on the LMS way more than usual, even though I tried to stay consistent otherwise.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or if discussion posts had replies. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd log in specifically to access video lectures and listen to them. Sometimes I'd just feel anxious about missing something important, so I'd check even when I knew nothing new was posted. That anxiety kept me logging in pretty frequently.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS kind of became automatic for me, especially in the mornings. I'd wake up, grab coffee, and just open it up to see what was happening. The discussion forums became a regular thing too because I actually enjoyed the peer interactions. It became habit after the first few weeks when I realized how much I relied on it to feel connected to the course.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd replay sections multiple times to really absorb the information. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing other students' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. Those peer interactions, all 48 of them, genuinely helped me process material in a way reading alone couldn't.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based supplementary materials. They just didn't work for how my brain processes information. I'd skim them at best or ignore them entirely, which probably hurt my exam performance. I knew they were there but kept telling myself I'd get to them later, and later never really came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually hear someone explaining concepts or if it involved back-and-forth with other students. Passive reading felt like a waste because nothing stuck. If I could listen and engage, even just posting in discussions, it felt productive. The immediate sense that I understood something better made it worthwhile.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always justify, even though I knew they might help. Required ones had this weight to them that pushed me to complete them no matter what. With optional stuff, my anxiety would kick in about whether I was spending time wisely, so I'd often skip them to focus on what was graded. It's frustrating because some optional content was probably really helpful.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded a ton of audio files and lecture recordings to listen to offline. I'd study on walks or during commutes where I couldn't be online. I also used some external podcasts and YouTube videos that explained similar concepts in different ways. A lot of my learning happened with earbuds in, away from any screen the LMS could track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing downloaded lectures or listening to recordings I'd saved, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts out loud to myself, which sounds weird but really helps me as an auditory learner. My actual study time was way higher than what my login patterns would suggest because so much happened offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture, watch it once online so it registered, then replay it offline three or four more times over the following days. The LMS only saw that one initial view, but I was engaging with that content repeatedly. It was more convenient and helped me focus without the distractions of being online.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My anxiety spiked badly, and even though I was putting in hours, nothing felt like it was working. I'd sit there listening to lectures but my mind would race about whether I was going to fail. The CBT sessions helped me manage that somewhat, but there were definitely weeks where engaging felt like pushing through mud.
Q13: Fatigue hit me hard sometimes, especially when I was anxious and not sleeping well. I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but just scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities weren't huge for me since I don't do extracurriculars, but mental exhaustion from anxiety definitely affected how meaningfully I could engage with the material.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but I was completely zoned out, just going through the motions. My mind would be somewhere else, worrying about exams or just feeling overwhelmed. I'd technically be logged in and watching, but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions felt wasted even though they'd look productive in the data.
Q15: I avoided exam prep materials for way too long because they triggered my anxiety. I knew studying for exams was crucial, but every time I tried, I'd feel this wave of dread about failing. So I'd focus on assignments instead since those felt more manageable and I could complete them successfully. It's probably why my assignment scores were decent but my exam score was terrible.
Q16: Deadlines were everything for my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'm logged in constantly, checking forums, rewatching lectures, making sure everything was submitted. Without that external pressure, I'd still study but more casually. The deadline created this urgency that cut through my usual anxiety paralysis and forced me to act.
Q17: Feedback was honestly a mixed bag for me. Positive feedback on assignments motivated me to keep going and made me feel like maybe I could succeed. But critical feedback, even constructive stuff, would sometimes send my anxiety through the roof. I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did get good feedback, I'd engage more confidently afterward.
Q18: The course structure with lots of video content worked well for my learning style, which kept me engaged. But the weight on exams versus assignments really hurt me since I did well on the continuous work but bombed the exam. If the structure had valued consistent participation more heavily, my grade would have reflected my actual effort better.
Q19: Having audio and video options made engagement way easier for me. The discussion forums being active also helped because I thrive on that interaction. What made it harder was when important information was only in text format or when exam content felt disconnected from what we'd been doing in assignments. That disconnect really threw me off.
Q20: I can relate to that pattern actually. Sometimes you're in the LMS trying to understand things, clicking around, watching videos, participating in discussions, but when it comes to the final submission, something doesn't translate. For me, it was like all that activity didn't prepare me for the exam format. High engagement doesn't always mean you've cracked the code on what's being assessed.
Q21: That's kind of the opposite of me, but I get it. Some people can read material once, understand it deeply, and perform well without needing all the repetition and interaction I need. They're probably more efficient learners or the format just clicks for them. The LMS would miss that they're actually highly capable students who just don't need the platform as much.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns pretty accurately, like when you're most active, what you click on, how often you participate in discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement completely. They can't tell if you're actually understanding or just going through motions. They also miss all the offline work and the emotional state behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, connecting it to things I already know, and feeling some investment in understanding it. It's not just showing up or completing tasks. It's that mental state where I'm curious and trying to make sense of things. Even when I'm anxious, if I'm genuinely wrestling with concepts, that's engagement to me.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything, or how the CBT sessions actually helped me keep going when I wanted to give up. It wouldn't capture all those hours listening to downloaded lectures on walks, or the genuine surprise I felt when I still failed despite all my effort. The data shows activity, but it doesn't show the person behind it struggling and trying their best.
